preFix.title: Air-dry clay spoon rest
preFix.subtitle: A slab-formed rest that catches drips and keeps the worktop clean
preFix.excerpt: A spoon rest is one of the most practical items a hand-builder can produce: a single rolled slab shaped around a curve, dried to hold its form, and sealed for light kitchen use. This version uses air-dry clay and takes less than an hour of hands-on time.
preFix.sourceNotes: Technique derived from first principles. Slab-forming methods referenced from Encyclopedia Britannica 11th edition (1911) pottery entry.

content.text: A spoon rest is the ideal first slab project: one piece, no joining, and a practical result that stays in daily use. This version is formed from a single rolled
slab

of air-dry clay, shaped while soft over a curved surface, and dried to hold a shallow cradle that catches drips. No kiln is required. The finished rest is sealed with diluted PVA, making it wipe-clean for light kitchen use as a utensil holder.

materials.name: PVA glue diluted 50/50 with water (for sealing)

content.text: Preparation

Condition 150 g of air-dry clay by pressing and folding it for two minutes until the texture is uniform and smooth. Work on a cloth or non-stick board. Cut two slab guides from folded card or use two wooden rails at 6 mm thickness to ensure an even roll. Find a curved surface to drape the slab over while it dries: a rounded jar, a large spoon, or a cardboard tube all work. Lightly dust the curved surface with cornflour to prevent adhesion.
Rolling the slab

Place the conditioned clay between two slab guides on your work surface. Roll from the centre outward in even strokes, rotating the slab a quarter-turn between rolls to keep the thickness consistent. Aim for 6 mm thick throughout.

Once the slab is flat and smooth, cut the outline with the fettling knife. An oval approximately 18 cm long and 9 cm wide is a practical size for a wooden spoon. Hold the knife nearly vertical and cut in one continuous line rather than sawing.
Use the needle tool to pierce a small drainage notch in one short end if you want the rest to be self-draining. A single 4 mm slot cut flush to the bottom face is enough.
Smooth the cut edges with a damp pottery sponge, wiping in short strokes along each edge to round them slightly. Sharp edges chip in use.
Forming the cradle shape

Lift the cut slab and lay it over your chosen curved surface so that the long axis sits across the curve. The clay should sag about 10 mm at the centre, enough to cradle a spoon handle without tipping, not so deep that it is difficult to place the spoon. Adjust the drape by raising or lowering the support. Leave the clay to stiffen on the form for two to three hours. Do not move it until it reaches the
leather-hard
stage.
Refining at leather-hard

Once the slab holds its curved shape without distorting, remove it from the support. Trim any uneven edges with the fettling knife. Use the optional wooden rib to smooth the upper surface in gentle circular motions. The rib compresses the clay surface and closes any small surface cracks. If a small crack has opened at the rim, press it gently closed with your fingertip and smooth over it with a damp sponge.
Drying and finishing
Set the rest on a flat surface and allow it to dry fully to
bone-dry

: at least 24 hours, longer in humid conditions. Do not rush this stage over a heat source; uneven drying causes warping. When the rest feels room-temperature to the touch on the underside and no damp patches are visible, it is ready to seal.

Apply two coats of PVA glue diluted 50/50 with water, allowing each coat to dry completely before the next. Use a foam brush or a wide, soft brush to apply evenly. The sealed surface will be slightly glossy but wipe-clean. This rest is suitable as a utensil holder that catches drips. It is not food-contact safe and should not be submerged in water.
Variations
Press a leaf into the surface before forming the cradle shape for an impressed botanical pattern on the upper face.
Cut the slab in a fish or leaf shape rather than an oval. Simple silhouette shapes cut cleanly with a template traced from card.
For a set, cut three matching ovals and stack them slightly offset during drying over the same curved form.

items.fix: Dry more slowly under a loosely draped cloth. Keep the rest on the support until it holds its shape unassisted for at least 30 minutes after removal
items.cause: The rest was removed from the curved support before leather-hard, or dried too quickly on one side

items.fix: Keep edges at 5-6 mm minimum. Cover with a light cloth for the first 12 hours to slow the drying at the thinner rim
items.cause: The clay was over-conditioned and the edges too thin, or the drying was too rapid

items.fix: Allow the rest to dry for a further 24 hours, then sand lightly with fine-grade paper before re-sealing with a thinner coat
items.cause: PVA applied too thickly, or the clay was not fully bone-dry before sealing
